title: 6.2.3 Nitrogen Cycle
description: Nitrogen cycle — every fact from the Grade 10 Biology textbook.

questions.question: Proteins are made up of which elements?
options: C, H, O, N, and S
questions.correctAnswer: C, H, O, N, and S

questions.question: What is the immediate result of photosynthesis?
What element transforms carbohydrate into protein?
How is nitrogen made available to plants?
options: From the soil through the nitrogen cycle
questions.correctAnswer: From the soil through the nitrogen cycle

questions.question: The nitrogen cycle is a repeating pathway through which components of the ecosystem?
Which biotic components are involved in the nitrogen cycle?
Which abiotic components are involved in the nitrogen cycle?
What are the major processes involved in cycling nitrogen?
options: Nitrogen fixation, ammonification, nitrification, assimilation, and denitrification
questions.correctAnswer: Nitrogen fixation, ammonification, nitrification, assimilation, and denitrification

questions.question: What percentage of the atmosphere is nitrogen gas ($N_2$)?
Are most living organisms able to incorporate atmospheric nitrogen ($N_2$) directly?
What usable forms must $N_2$ be fixed into?
What is nitrogen fixation?
options: The release of $N_2$ into the atmosphere
The absorption of nitrate by plants

questions.question: What are the three ways nitrogen fixation can occur?
options: Biologically (mediated by bacteria), non-biologically (atmospheric fixation by lightning), and industrial fixation
questions.correctAnswer: Biologically (mediated by bacteria), non-biologically (atmospheric fixation by lightning), and industrial fixation

questions.question: What are the two categories of biological $N_2$ fixation?
options: Symbiotic nitrogen fixation and non-symbiotic fixation
questions.correctAnswer: Symbiotic nitrogen fixation and non-symbiotic fixation

questions.question: Symbiotic nitrogen fixation is carried out in the root nodules of which type of plants?
options: Leguminous plants (such as peas, beans, and peanuts)
questions.correctAnswer: Leguminous plants (such as peas, beans, and peanuts)

questions.question: Which group of soil bacteria carries out symbiotic nitrogen fixation in legume root nodules?
What do rhizobia form with leguminous plants?
options: A symbiotic association that forms root nodules where $N_2$ is reduced into ammonia
questions.correctAnswer: A symbiotic association that forms root nodules where $N_2$ is reduced into ammonia

questions.question: What enzyme catalyzes the nitrogen fixation reaction?
What is the balanced equation for nitrogen fixation?
What are the ammonium ions produced by nitrogen fixation used for in the legume plant?
options: Synthesizing amino acids, the building units of protein
questions.correctAnswer: Synthesizing amino acids, the building units of protein

questions.question: What is nitrous oxide ($N_2O$) an intermediate product of?
options: Microbial denitrification in the nitrogen cycle
questions.correctAnswer: Microbial denitrification in the nitrogen cycle

questions.question: How does the global warming potential of $N_2O$ compare to $CO_2$?
options: It is over 300 times greater than $CO_2$
questions.correctAnswer: It is over 300 times greater than $CO_2$

questions.question: What is the major source of $N_2O$ emissions?
options: Excessive use of synthetic N fertilizer in agricultural systems
questions.correctAnswer: Excessive use of synthetic N fertilizer in agricultural systems

questions.question: How can biological $N_2$ fixation by rhizobia help the environment?
options: By serving as an alternative to synthetic N fertilizer and mitigating $N_2O$ emissions
questions.correctAnswer: By serving as an alternative to synthetic N fertilizer and mitigating $N_2O$ emissions

questions.question: Which bacteria carry out non-symbiotic nitrogen fixation?
options: Free living soil bacteria of the genera Azotobacter and Klebsiella
questions.correctAnswer: Free living soil bacteria of the genera Azotobacter and Klebsiella

questions.question: What do free-living nitrogen-fixing bacteria reduce nitrogen gas into?
What is nitrification?
options: A microbial process by which ammonia is sequentially oxidized first to nitrite and then to nitrate
questions.correctAnswer: A microbial process by which ammonia is sequentially oxidized first to nitrite and then to nitrate

questions.question: Which bacteria oxidize ammonia to nitrite in the first step of nitrification?
What is the chemical equation for the first step of nitrification?
Which bacteria oxidize nitrite to nitrate in the second step of nitrification?
What is the chemical equation for the second step of nitrification?
In what inorganic forms do plants commonly absorb nitrogen?
Which form of nitrogen do plants preferentially take up?
Why do plants prefer ammonium ($NH_4$) over nitrate ($NO_3$)?
options: Because the energy expenditure for $NH_4$ assimilation is much lower than for $NO_3$
questions.correctAnswer: Because the energy expenditure for $NH_4$ assimilation is much lower than for $NO_3$

questions.question: How can roots absorb nitrogen ions?
After absorption, where are nitrogen ions transported in the plant?
options: To the stem and leaves through the root xylem
questions.correctAnswer: To the stem and leaves through the root xylem

questions.question: What are ammonium and nitrate used for after being assimilated by plants?
options: Synthesis of amino acids which are polymerized into proteins
questions.correctAnswer: Synthesis of amino acids which are polymerized into proteins

questions.question: What is ammonification?
options: Conversion of reduced organic matter having an amine group to reduced inorganic nitrogen ($NH_4$) through microorganisms
questions.correctAnswer: Conversion of reduced organic matter having an amine group to reduced inorganic nitrogen ($NH_4$) through microorganisms

questions.question: What is the general formula for an amine group?
Ammonification is an intermediary step between which processes?
options: Depolymerization of large organic molecules and the nitrification process
questions.correctAnswer: Depolymerization of large organic molecules and the nitrification process

questions.question: What is the sequence from dead organic matter to nitrate?
options: Dead organic matter -> Depolymerization -> Ammonification -> Nitrification
questions.correctAnswer: Dead organic matter -> Depolymerization -> Ammonification -> Nitrification

questions.question: What is denitrification?
options: Conversion of nitrate to nitrogen gas ($N_2$), removing bioavailable nitrogen and returning it to the atmosphere
questions.correctAnswer: Conversion of nitrate to nitrogen gas ($N_2$), removing bioavailable nitrogen and returning it to the atmosphere

questions.question: What is the sequential conversion in denitrification?
Is denitrification an aerobic or anaerobic process?
Where does denitrification mostly occur?
options: In waterlogged soils (anoxic), layers of sediments in lakes and oceans
questions.correctAnswer: In waterlogged soils (anoxic), layers of sediments in lakes and oceans

questions.question: Which bacteria are responsible for symbiotic nitrogen fixation in legume root nodules?
Which bacteria are free-living nitrogen fixers in soil?
Which bacteria are involved in nitrification?
Which organisms are involved in ammonification?
options: Ammonifying bacteria (aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, fungi)
questions.correctAnswer: Ammonifying bacteria (aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, fungi)

questions.question: Which bacteria are involved in denitrification?
What is nitrogen the most important component of in an organism?
What does DNA contain that guides all activities in living organisms?
What does DNA hold instructions for making?
options: All proteins, both structural and functional
questions.correctAnswer: All proteins, both structural and functional

questions.question: What does DNA stand for?
In which cells is DNA found?
options: In all prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells
questions.correctAnswer: In all prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells

questions.question: What does DNA code for?
options: Genetic information for the transmission of inherited traits
questions.correctAnswer: Genetic information for the transmission of inherited traits

questions.question: A DNA molecule is composed of a long chain of what?
What does each nucleotide consist of?
options: Amino acid, phosphate, and nitrogen base
A five-carbon sugar (deoxyribose), a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
questions.correctAnswer: A five-carbon sugar (deoxyribose), a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base

questions.question: What are examples of functional proteins?
options: Catalytic (enzymes), storage (albumin, casein), transport (hemoglobin), protective (antibodies, blood clotting factors)
questions.correctAnswer: Catalytic (enzymes), storage (albumin, casein), transport (hemoglobin), protective (antibodies, blood clotting factors)

questions.question: What are examples of structural proteins?
options: Bones, muscles, cell membrane, and skin
questions.correctAnswer: Bones, muscles, cell membrane, and skin

questions.question: Can plants directly use atmospheric nitrogen ($N_2$)?
options: No, plants cannot directly use atmospheric nitrogen
questions.correctAnswer: No, plants cannot directly use atmospheric nitrogen

questions.question: What inorganic forms of nitrogen do plants use?
What do soil bacteria like nitrogen-fixing bacteria and nitrifying bacteria provide to plants?
What does ammonifying bacteria do by decomposing dead organic matter?
How do soil bacteria affect soil fertility?
options: They increase soil fertility, resulting in increased plant growth and agricultural productivity
questions.correctAnswer: They increase soil fertility, resulting in increased plant growth and agricultural productivity

questions.question: What can nitrogen-fixing bacteria be used as due to their capacity for fixing nitrogen?
What advantages do biofertilizers from nitrogen-fixing bacteria offer?
options: They improve soil structure and serve as a continuous source of natural fertilizer without being leached easily
They only work for one season
questions.correctAnswer: They improve soil structure and serve as a continuous source of natural fertilizer without being leached easily

questions.question: Which soil organism is nicknamed 'Friend of farmers'?
What are earthworms one of the major decomposers of?
What does the mutual action of earthworms and microbes bring about?
options: Faster decomposition of organic matter and enhanced cycling of nutrients
questions.correctAnswer: Faster decomposition of organic matter and enhanced cycling of nutrients

questions.question: What is a good indicator of fertile soil?
What do earthworm burrows do to the soil?
options: Improve soil structure, particularly soil porosity which increases air and water holding capacity
questions.correctAnswer: Improve soil structure, particularly soil porosity which increases air and water holding capacity

questions.question: What are bio-fertilizers?
options: Microbial preparations containing living cells of microorganisms that mobilize nutrients from unusable to usable form
questions.correctAnswer: Microbial preparations containing living cells of microorganisms that mobilize nutrients from unusable to usable form

questions.question: What are the characteristics of bio-fertilizers?
options: Expensive, toxic, and harmful to environment

questions.question: What do bio-fertilizers serve as a good substitute for?
What do bio-fertilizers increase?
options: Crop yield, ensure food security, and sustainable agriculture
questions.correctAnswer: Crop yield, ensure food security, and sustainable agriculture

questions.question: What is vermicompost?
options: Organic fertilizer obtained by using worms (commonly earthworms) that decompose food scraps and organic waste
questions.correctAnswer: Organic fertilizer obtained by using worms (commonly earthworms) that decompose food scraps and organic waste

questions.question: Why do organic farmers usually grow legumes such as peas and beans?
options: For dual purpose: harvest the legumes as a crop and use fixed nitrogen in soil for non-legume crops
questions.correctAnswer: For dual purpose: harvest the legumes as a crop and use fixed nitrogen in soil for non-legume crops

questions.question: Why do organic farmers grow non-legume crops after harvesting legumes?
options: To take advantage of all nitrogen fixed and accumulated in the soil and roots of the legumes
questions.correctAnswer: To take advantage of all nitrogen fixed and accumulated in the soil and roots of the legumes

questions.question: What is the term for the practice of growing legumes followed by non-legumes to utilize fixed nitrogen?
